one_line_takeaway: LLM-HyPZ is a zero-shot LLM classification + embedding-clustering + LLM-summarization pipeline that mined 1,742 hardware CVEs from a 114,836-entry 2021-2024 corpus and demonstrably fed 411 of the 1,026 CVEs used in MITRE's real CWE MIHW 2025 update, evidenced by LLaMA 3.3 70B reaching 99.5% accuracy on a 200-example hardware/software CVE classification validation set.
key_finding: On the 200-example validation set, LLaMA 3.3 70B achieved 99.5% accuracy on zero-shot hardware-vs-software CVE classification, described by the authors as 'near-perfect,' with GPT-4-Turbo and GPT-4o also reported as achieving near-perfect accuracy; smaller/older models degraded sharply (GPT-3.5-Turbo below 0.75 accuracy, LLaMA 3.2 3B below 0.65 accuracy). Applying the selected LLaMA 3.3 70B classifier to the full 2021-2024 CVE corpus (114,836 entries) surfaced 1,742 hardware-related CVEs, which K-means (K=5, via Elbow method) partitioned into five thematically coherent clusters, each given an LLM-generated (GPT-5) topic label (e.g., 'Physical Access Exploitation of Firmware and Hardware Control,' 'Memory Corruption in IoT and Mobile Connectivity Systems,' 'Firmware Privilege Escalation and Denial-of-Service via BIOS Exploitation'). Real-world validation: of the 1,026 CVEs that MITRE's CWE MIHW 2025 working group ultimately used after human review, 411 were CVEs originally surfaced by the LLM-HyPZ pipeline -- an external, independently-verified downstream usage signal rather than a self-reported metric.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is LLM-HyPZ, a three-stage pipeline for zero-shot mining of hardware-related vulnerabilities from the CVE corpus: (1) an LLM (used as a zero-shot binary classifier via a persona/reflection prompt) labels each CVE description as hardware- or software-related; (2) the hardware-flagged CVEs are embedded with a contextualized embedding model and partitioned with K-means (K chosen via the Elbow method); (3) each cluster is characterized by top-r frequent uni/bi/trigrams and then summarized into a human-readable topic label by a second LLM call. The authors applied this pipeline to the 2021-2024 CVE corpus (114,836 entries), producing 1,742 hardware-related CVEs distilled into 5 clusters/topics, and report that the pipeline's output was actually used to narrow the candidate search space for MITRE's CWE Most Important Hardware Weaknesses (MIHW) 2025 update, contributing 411 of the 1,026 CVEs that human experts ultimately incorporated into that downstream analysis.
threat_model: This is not an attack/defense paper and has no adversarial threat model. It is a data-mining / knowledge-discovery paper whose 'threat' framing is descriptive: hardware vulnerabilities (privilege escalation via firmware/BIOS, memory corruption in IoT/mobile, physical-access exploits, memory corruption leading to arbitrary code execution/information disclosure) are treated as the object of study drawn from public CVE records, not as vulnerabilities the authors discover, exploit, or defend against directly. There is no discussion of an adversary model, attack surface, or defensive mechanism in the security-engineering sense; the contribution is a classification/clustering/summarization methodology applied to existing public vulnerability disclosures.
limitations: The authors state these limitations explicitly in their conclusion: (1) contextual ambiguity between hardware, firmware, and software in CVE text is nontrivial, and borderline cases still require human validation despite high classification accuracy; (2) reliance on high-capacity models (LLaMA 3.3 70B, GPT-4o) introduces significant computational and financial overhead that may limit practical large-scale deployment; (3) the K-means clustering approach assumes a fixed number of clusters (K=5, chosen once via the Elbow method) and may not capture the evolving taxonomy of hardware weaknesses. Additional grounding-level caveats not raised by the authors but visible in the text: the 200-example validation set is small and manually curated (not necessarily representative of corpus-wide class balance, since the full corpus is only ~1.5% hardware-related by their own count of 1,742/114,836); the embedding model used differs between the methodology description (text-embedding-3-large) and the reported large-scale-corpus run (text-embedding-ada-002), an internal inconsistency in the paper; noise removal (brand/product names) before summarization was done manually, limiting full automation; and no ablation isolates the individual contribution of the classification stage vs. the clustering/summarization stage to the MIHW-relevant CVE recall.

datasets_used: DCVE_Validation: 200 manually labeled CVEs (100 hardware-related, 100 software-related), detailed in Appendix A/Table III
1,742 hardware-related CVEs identified from the production corpus, used for embedding/clustering
MITRE CWE MIHW 2025 working-group set of 1,026 human-validated CVEs (external downstream dataset), listed in Appendix B/Table IV
quantitative_results: LLaMA 3.3 70B: 99.5% accuracy on the 200-example HW/SW zero-shot classification validation set (best-performing model, selected for production run)
GPT-4-Turbo and GPT-4o: reported as achieving 'near-perfect' accuracy alongside LLaMA 3.3 70B
GPT-3.5-Turbo: accuracy below 0.75 on the same validation task
LLaMA 3.2 3B: accuracy below 0.65 on the same validation task
K=5 clusters selected via Elbow method for the 1,742 hardware CVE embeddings
411 of 1,026 MIHW-2025 candidate CVEs (about 40%) were originally surfaced by the LLM-HyPZ pipeline
baselines_compared: Seven LLMs benchmarked on the zero-shot HW/SW classification task (named in text: LLaMA 3.3 70B, GPT-4-Turbo, GPT-4o, GPT-3.5-Turbo, LLaMA 3.2 3B; remaining two visible only in Fig. 5, not extractable as text)

Traditional/prior CVE-mining approaches discussed only in Related Work as prior art, not re-run as experimental baselines in this paper: rule-based CPE extraction with Bi-LSTM+CRF (Wareus & Hell), LDA topic modeling (Neuhaus & Zimmermann), attribute-enrichment framework for OS vulnerabilities (Bhurtel & Rawat) -- these are NOT quantitatively compared against LLM-HyPZ in the paper's own experiments, only contrasted qualitatively
